37.4,-98.3.p: This I learnt from the man Johann, whom I was compelled to trust and send back to Zenda, where, by the way, Rupert Hentzau had him soundly flogged for daring to smirch the morals of Zenda by staying out all night in the pursuits of love.

37.4,-98.3.p: “Wait till we are married,” said I, smiling. Not the least peculiar result of the truce and of the secrecy which dictated it was that the town of Zenda became in the day-time--I would not have trusted far to its protection by night--a sort of neutral zone, where both parties could safely go; and I, riding down one day with Flavia and Sapt, had an en

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 15

I Talk with a Tempter
37.4,-98.3.p: I recognized the Head of the Strelsau Police. “Your Majesty’s ordinance as to duelling is receiving our best attention,” he assured me. If the best attention involved his presence in Zenda, I determined at once to dispense with it.

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 15

I Talk with a Tempter
37.4,-98.3.p: If the best attention involved his presence in Zenda, I determined at once to dispense with it. “Is that what brings you to Zenda, Prefect?” I asked.

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 15

I Talk with a Tempter
37.4,-98.3.p: “A young countryman of his, sire--a man of some position--is missing. His friends have not heard from him for two months, and there is reason to believe that he was last seen in Zenda.” Flavia was paying little attention.

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 16

A Desperate Plan
37.4,-98.3.p: CHAPTER 16 A Desperate Plan As I had ridden publicly in Zenda, and had talked there with Rupert Hentzau, of course all pretence of illness was at an end.

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 16

A Desperate Plan
37.4,-98.3.p: I marked the effect on the garrison of Zenda: they ceased to be seen abroad; and any of my men who went near the Castle reported that the utmost vigilance prevailed there.

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 16

A Desperate Plan
37.4,-98.3.p: Yet his life hung in the balance; and I was still strong and whole and free. Wherefore great gloom reigned at Zenda; and save when they quarrelled, to which they were very prone, they hardly spoke.

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 17

Young Rupert’s Midnight Diversions
37.4,-98.3.p: At twelve o’clock, Sapt’s command left the chateau of Tarlenheim and struck off to the right, riding by unfrequented roads, and avoiding the town of Zenda.

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 19

Face to Face in the Forest
37.4,-98.3.p: Unwounded and vigorous, he gained on me at every step; but, forgetting everything in the world except him and my thirst for his blood, I pressed on, and soon the deep shades of the forest of Zenda engulfed us both, pursued and pursuer. It was three o’clock now, and day was dawning.

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 19

Face to Face in the Forest
37.4,-98.3.p: “Thanks to the most gallant gentleman that lives,” said he softly, “the King is alive!” The little farm-girl stood by us, weeping for fright and wide-eyed for wonder; for she had seen me at Zenda; and was not I, pallid, dripping, foul, and bloody as I was--yet was not I the King?

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 20

The Prisoner and the King
37.4,-98.3.p: His triumph she did not desire, for she loathed his crime, and loathed yet more fiercely what would be the prize of it--his marriage with his cousin, Princess Flavia. At Zenda new forces came into play--the lust and daring of young Rupert. He was caught by her beauty, perhaps; perhaps it was enough for him that she belonged to another man, and that she hated him.

37.4,-98.3.p: And as she drove up the hill, with the Marshal riding by the wheel and still imploring her to return in obedience to the King’s orders, Fritz von Tarlenheim, with the prisoner of Zenda, came to the edge of the forest.

If love were all!
37.4,-98.3.p: Some said that the prisoner of Zenda was dead; some, that he had vanished yet alive; some, that he was a friend who had served the King well in some adventure in England; others, that he had discovered the Duke’s plots, and had therefor

37.4,-98.3.ch:
CHAPTER 21

If love were all!
37.4,-98.3.p: Doubtless I should have thought more of it, had there not echoed through the air, from the towers of Zenda that we were leaving far away, into my ears and into my heart the cry of a woman’s love--“Rudolf! Rudolf!
